title: HTML Intro
content:
HTML Introduction

What is HTML?

HTML stands for Hyper Text Markup Language. It is the standard language used to create web pages. It describes the structure of a webpage using elements, telling the browser how to display content.

title: Basic Structure
content:
HTML Basic Structure

The Boilerplate

Every HTML document requires a basic structure. Here is the standard boilerplate code you will use to start any web page:

HTML Document
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <title>Page Title</title>
</head>
<body>

  <h1>My First Heading</h1>
  <p>My first paragraph.</p>

</body>
</html>

title: HTML Elements
content:
HTML Elements

Understanding Elements

An HTML element is defined by a start tag, some content, and an end tag.

Element Syntax
<p>This is a paragraph</p>
<h1>This is a heading</h1>

Empty Elements

Some elements are self-closing and do not have an end tag. These are called empty elements, like the line break <br> and the image tag <img>.

title: HTML Attributes
content:
HTML Attributes

What are Attributes?

Attributes provide additional information about elements. They are always specified in the start tag and usually come in name/value pairs like name="value".

The href Attribute
<!-- The href attribute specifies the URL of the page the link goes to -->
<a href="https://www.google.com" target="_blank">Visit Google in a new tab!</a>

Common attributes include: href (links), src (images), alt (alternative text), id, and class.

title: HTML Headings
content:
HTML Headings

Heading Tags

HTML headings are defined with the <h1> to <h6> tags. <h1> defines the most important heading, while <h6> defines the least important heading.

Heading Hierarchy
<h1>Heading 1 (Main Title)</h1>
<h2>Heading 2 (Subtitle)</h2>
<h3>Heading 3</h3>
<h4>Heading 4</h4>
<h5>Heading 5</h5>
<h6>Heading 6</h6>

title: HTML Paragraphs
content:
HTML Paragraphs

The Paragraph Tag

The <p> tag defines a paragraph. Browsers automatically add a single blank line before and after each <p> element.

Paragraph Formatting
<p>This is a paragraph.</p>
<p>This is another paragraph.
Notice how the browser adds space between us automatically.</p>

<p>If you want a line break<br>without starting a new paragraph, use the br tag.</p>

title: HTML Links
content:
HTML Links

Hyperlinks

Links are created using the anchor <a> tag. The destination address is specified in the href attribute.

Link Example
<h2>HTML Links</h2>
<p>Click the link below to visit an example website:</p>

<a href="https://www.example.com" target="_blank">Visit Example.com</a>

title: HTML Images
content:
HTML Images

Displaying Images

Images are added using the empty <img> tag. It requires the src attribute (source file) and should always include the alt attribute (alternative text for screen readers).

Image Syntax
<h2>A Random Landscape</h2>
<p>Here is an image loaded from an external URL:</p>

<img src="https://picsum.photos/400/250" alt="Random beautiful landscape" width="400" style="border-radius: 8px;">

title: HTML Lists
content:
HTML Lists

Unordered & Ordered Lists

Unordered lists use bullets (<ul>), while ordered lists use numbers (<ol>). The list items inside are defined with <li>.

List Examples
<h3>My Grocery List (Unordered)</h3>
<ul>
  <li>Milk</li>
  <li>Bread</li>
  <li>Eggs</li>
</ul>

<h3>Top 3 Movies (Ordered)</h3>
<ol>
  <li>The Matrix</li>
  <li>Inception</li>
  <li>Interstellar</li>
</ol>

title: HTML Tables
content:
HTML Tables

Creating Data Tables

Tables are defined with <table>. Rows are added with <tr>, headers with <th>, and data cells with <td>.

Table Syntax
<table border="1" style="width:100%; text-align:left; border-collapse: collapse;">
  <tr style="background-color: #f2f2f2;">
    <th style="padding: 8px;">Name</th>
    <th style="padding: 8px;">Age</th>
    <th style="padding: 8px;">Profession</th>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td style="padding: 8px;">John Doe</td>
    <td style="padding: 8px;">25</td>
    <td style="padding: 8px;">Web Developer</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td style="padding: 8px;">Jane Smith</td>
    <td style="padding: 8px;">30</td>
    <td style="padding: 8px;">Designer</td>
  </tr>
</table>

title: HTML Forms
content:
HTML Forms

Collecting User Input

HTML forms are used to collect user input, such as names, emails, and passwords. The data is usually sent to a server for processing.

Basic Form Example
<h2>Contact Us</h2>
<form action="/submit">
  <label for="fname">First name:</label><br>
  <input type="text" id="fname" name="fname" value="John"><br><br>
  <label for="lname">Last name:</label><br>
  <input type="text" id="lname" name="lname" value="Doe"><br><br>
  <input type="submit" value="Submit">
</form>

title: HTML Input Types
content:
HTML Input Types

Different Input Fields

The <input> element is the most used form element. It can be displayed in many ways, depending on the type attribute.

Input Types Demo
<form>
  <label>Text:</label> <input type="text" placeholder="Enter text"><br><br>
  <label>Password:</label> <input type="password"><br><br>
  <label>Number:</label> <input type="number" min="1" max="10"><br><br>
  <label>Date:</label> <input type="date"><br><br>
  <label>Color Check:</label> <input type="color">
</form>

title: HTML Form Attributes
content:
HTML Form Attributes

Controlling Forms

The <form> element has important attributes like action (where to send data) and method (how to send it: GET or POST). Inputs also have attributes like required or placeholder.

Form Attributes
<form action="/submit_page.php" method="post">
  <label>Email (Required):</label><br>
  <input type="email" name="email" placeholder="e.g. hello@example.com" required><br><br>
  <input type="submit" value="Register">
</form>

title: Semantic Elements
content:
HTML Semantic Elements

What are Semantic Elements?

Semantic elements clearly describe their meaning to both the browser and the developer. Instead of using generic <div> tags everywhere, HTML5 introduced descriptive tags.



Semantic Structure
<header style="background-color:#cbd5e1; padding:10px;">
  <h1>My Website</h1>
</header>

<nav style="background-color:#334155; color:white; padding:10px;">
  Home | About | Contact
</nav>

<section style="padding:20px;">
  <article>
    <h2>Breaking News</h2>
    <p>Semantic HTML makes web pages more accessible!</p>
  </article>
</section>

<footer style="background-color:#cbd5e1; padding:10px; text-align:center;">
  © 2026 My Website
</footer>

title: HTML Div and Span
content:
HTML Div and Span

Containers

The <div> tag is a block-level container used to group elements. The <span> tag is an inline container used to style parts of a text.

Div vs Span
<div style="background-color: lightblue; padding: 20px;">
  <h3>I am a block-level Div!</h3>
  <p>This is a paragraph inside the div. 
  I want to make <span style="color: red; font-weight: bold;">just these words</span> stand out using a span tag.</p>
</div>

title: HTML Classes & IDs
content:
HTML Classes and IDs

Styling Hooks

The class attribute applies styles to multiple elements, while the id attribute specifies a unique identifier for a single element.

Class vs ID Example
<style>
  .highlight {
    background-color: yellow;
  }
  #unique-header {
    color: blue;
    text-decoration: underline;
  }
</style>

<h2 id="unique-header">I have a unique ID!</h2>
<p class="highlight">This paragraph has the highlight class.</p>
<p class="highlight">This paragraph ALSO has the highlight class!</p>

title: HTML Iframes
content:
HTML Iframes

Embedding Pages

An <iframe> is used to display a web page within a web page. It requires a src attribute to point to the URL.

Iframe Example
<h2>Wikipedia inside an Iframe</h2>
<p>Below is a window showing another website:</p>

<iframe src="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTML" width="100%" height="300" style="border: 2px solid #ccc;"></iframe>

title: HTML Audio
content:
HTML Audio

Playing Sounds

The <audio> element is used to play an audio file. The controls attribute adds audio controls like play, pause, and volume.

Audio Example
<h3>Listen to this audio snippet:</h3>
<audio controls>
  <source src="https://www.w3schools.com/html/horse.ogg" type="audio/ogg">
  <source src="https://www.w3schools.com/html/horse.mp3" type="audio/mpeg">
  Your browser does not support the audio element.
</audio>

title: HTML Video
content:
HTML Video

Embedding Videos

The <video> element is used to show a video. Just like audio, adding the controls attribute is highly recommended so users can play or pause it.

Video Example
<h3>Watch this video:</h3>
<video width="400" controls>
  <source src="https://www.w3schools.com/html/mov_bbb.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser does not support HTML video.
</video>

title: HTML Meta Tags
content:
HTML Meta Tags

Document Metadata

The <meta> tag provides metadata about the HTML document, like character sets, page descriptions, keywords, and viewport settings (for responsive design). They always go inside the <head> element.

Common Meta Tags
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="description" content="Free Web tutorials">
  <meta name="keywords" content="HTML, CSS, JavaScript">
  <meta name="author" content="John Doe">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>Meta Tags Example</title>
</head>
<body>
  <p>Meta tags are invisible, but crucial for SEO and responsive design!</p>
</body>
</html>

title: Block vs Inline
content:
HTML Block and Inline Elements

Element Display Flow

Every HTML element has a default display value depending on what type of element it is. The two most common display values are block and inline.



Block vs Inline Example
<h3>Block Elements</h3>
<p>Block elements take up the full width available.</p>
<div style="background-color: lightblue; padding: 10px;">I am a block-level DIV element!</div>
<div style="background-color: lightcoral; padding: 10px;">I am another block-level DIV!</div>

<h3>Inline Elements</h3>
<p>Inline elements only take up as much width as necessary.</p>
<span style="background-color: lightgreen; padding: 10px;">I am an inline SPAN!</span>
<span style="background-color: yellow; padding: 10px;">I am another SPAN!</span>

title: HTML Comments
content:
HTML Comments

Documenting Code

Comments are not displayed by the browser, but they can help document your HTML source code. They are useful for hiding content temporarily or explaining complex code.

Comment Syntax
<!-- This is a comment. The browser will ignore it. -->
<p>This is a visible paragraph.</p>

<!-- 
  You can also write
  multi-line comments!
-->

<!-- <p>This paragraph is temporarily hidden!</p> -->

title: HTML Colors
content:
HTML Colors

Coloring Elements

Colors can be defined using predefined color names, HEX codes, or RGB values. You can apply them to text, backgrounds, and borders using the style attribute.

Color Formats
<h3 style="color: Tomato;">Tomato (Color Name)</h3>
<h3 style="color: #3CB371;">MediumSeaGreen (HEX)</h3>
<h3 style="color: rgb(255, 99, 71);">Tomato (RGB)</h3>

<p style="background-color: DodgerBlue; color: white; padding: 10px;">
  Background colors are cool too!
</p>

title: CSS Integration
content:
HTML CSS Integration

Adding Styles

CSS can be added to HTML in three ways: Inline (using the style attribute), Internal (using the <style> tag in the head), or External (linking a .css file).

Internal & Inline CSS
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <style>
    body { background-color: powderblue; }
    h1 { color: blue; }
    p { font-size: 18px; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>

  <h1>This heading uses Internal CSS</h1>
  <p>This paragraph uses Internal CSS.</p>
  <p style="color: red; font-weight: bold;">This paragraph overrides it with Inline CSS!</p>

</body>
</html>

title: JS Integration
content:
HTML JavaScript Integration

Adding Interactivity

JavaScript makes HTML pages more dynamic. You can add JS directly into your HTML file using the <script> tag, or link to an external .js file.

Script Tag Example
<h2>JavaScript in HTML</h2>
<p id="demo">Click the button to change this text.</p>

<button onclick="document.getElementById('demo').innerHTML = 'Hello JavaScript!'">
  Click Me!
</button>

<script>
  // You can also write JS logic right here!
  console.log("Page loaded.");
</script>

title: HTML Entities
content:
HTML Entities

Reserved Characters

Some characters are reserved in HTML. For example, you cannot use the less than (<) or greater than (>) signs directly in your text, because the browser might mix them with tags. Instead, we use entity codes.

Entity Examples
<p>Use &lt; to show a less than sign: <</p>
<p>Use &gt; to show a greater than sign: ></p>
<p>The ampersand sign looks like this: &</p>
<p>And here is the copyright symbol: ©</p>
<p>HTML    ignores    extra    spaces. Use &nbsp; to force a non-breaking    space!</p>

title: Symbols & Emojis
content:
HTML Symbols and Emojis

Unicode Characters

Many mathematical, technical, and currency symbols, as well as emojis, are not present on a normal keyboard. You can add them using their Unicode value.

Emoji & Symbol Rendering
<h2>HTML Symbols</h2>
<p>I will display a Euro sign: €</p>
<p>I will display a heart: ♥</p>

<h2>HTML Emojis!</h2>
<p>Grinning Face: 😀</p>
<p>Rocket: 🚀</p>
<p>Pizza: 🍕</p>

title: HTML File Paths
content:
HTML File Paths

Locating Files

A file path describes the location of a file in a web site's folder structure. It's used when linking to external files like images, CSS stylesheets, or JavaScript files.

    <li><strong>Absolute Path:</strong> A full URL to a file (e.g., <code>https://example.com/images/pic.jpg</code>).</li> <li><strong>Relative Path:</strong> A path relative to the current page (e.g., <code>/images/pic.jpg</code> or <code>../pic.jpg</code>).</li> </ul>
    Path Examples
    <!-- Absolute Path (Loads from the internet) -->
<img src="https://picsum.photos/200" alt="Random Image">

<!-- Relative Path (Looks inside your own project folder) -->
<!-- <img src="images/my-local-photo.jpg" alt="Local Image"> -->

title: Responsive Design
content:
HTML Responsive Design

Mobile-Friendly Pages

Web pages can be viewed using many different devices: desktops, tablets, and phones. Responsive web design is about creating pages that look good on all devices.

The Viewport Meta Tag
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <!-- This meta tag is CRITICAL for mobile responsiveness! -->
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <style>
    .responsive-img {
      max-width: 100%;
      height: auto;
    }
  </style>
</head>
<body>

  <h2>Responsive Image</h2>
  <p>Resize the browser window to see how the image scales down.</p>
  <img src="https://picsum.photos/800/400" alt="Nature" class="responsive-img">

</body>
</html>

title: Best Practices
content:
HTML Best Practices

Writing Clean Code

Follow these best practices to ensure your HTML is clean, accessible, and maintainable:

    <li><strong>Use Semantic Elements:</strong> Use <code><header></code>, <code><main></code>, and <code><footer></code> instead of endless divs.</li> <li><strong>Indent your Code:</strong> Proper indentation makes your code readable.</li> <li><strong>Use Lowercase Tags:</strong> Always write <code><p></code>, never <code><P></code>.</li> <li><strong>Always Add Alt Text:</strong> Screen readers rely on <code>alt</code> attributes on images.</li> </ul>
    A Clean Template
    <!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>Clean HTML</title>
</head>
<body>
  
  <header>
    <h1>Semantic & Clean</h1>
  </header>
  
  <main>
    <p>This is a perfectly structured, accessible HTML document!</p>
  </main>
  
</body>
</html>

title: HTML5 Intro
content:
HTML5 Introduction

What is HTML5?

HTML5 is the latest major version of HTML. It introduces new semantic elements, robust multimedia support, and powerful JavaScript APIs that turn the web into an application platform.

  • New semantic tags (<header>, <footer>, etc.)
  • Native Audio and Video support
  • Canvas and SVG for drawing graphics
  • Web Storage and Geolocation APIs

title: HTML5 Canvas
content:
HTML5 Canvas

Drawing Graphics

The <canvas> element is used to draw graphics on the fly via JavaScript. The canvas itself is just a container; you must use a script to actually draw the shapes, text, or images.

Canvas Example
<canvas id="myCanvas" width="200" height="100" style="border:2px solid #334155;"></canvas>

<script>
  var c = document.getElementById('myCanvas');
  var ctx = c.getContext('2d');
  ctx.fillStyle = '#ff4757';
  ctx.fillRect(10, 10, 150, 75);
</script>

title: HTML5 SVG
content:
HTML5 SVG

Scalable Vector Graphics

SVG defines vector-based graphics in XML format. Unlike Canvas (which is built with pixels), SVG images are built with math, meaning they never lose quality when zoomed or resized.



SVG Example
<svg width="200" height="100" style="background-color: #f1f5f9;">
  <circle cx="100" cy="50" r="40" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="4" fill="#00ffb3" />
</svg>

title: Drag and Drop
content:
HTML5 Drag and Drop

Interactive Elements

HTML5 natively supports drag and drop functionality. You can make almost any element draggable simply by adding the draggable="true" attribute.

Draggable Attribute
<div draggable="true" style="width:150px; height:100px; background-color:#38bdf8; color:white; padding:10px; cursor:grab; border-radius:8px; text-align:center; line-height:80px;">
  Drag me around!
</div>

Note: JavaScript is required to define exactly what happens when the element is dropped into a new container.

title: Web Storage
content:
HTML5 Web Storage

Browser Memory

Web Storage provides a way for web applications to store data locally within the user's browser. It replaces older, clunkier cookies. It offers localStorage (saves data forever) and sessionStorage (clears when the tab closes).

LocalStorage Example
<button onclick="saveName()">Save Name to Browser</button>
<button onclick="loadName()">Load Name</button>
<p id="output"></p>

<script>
  function saveName() {
    localStorage.setItem('name', 'Sanjith');
    alert('Name saved to LocalStorage!');
  }
  function loadName() {
    var name = localStorage.getItem('name');
    document.getElementById('output').innerText = "Hello, " + name;
  }
</script>

title: Geolocation
content:
HTML5 Geolocation

Finding the User

The HTML5 Geolocation API is used to get the geographical position of a user. Since this compromises privacy, the position is not available unless the user explicitly approves it.



Geolocation API
<button onclick="getLocation()">Get My Coordinates</button>
<p id="geo-output"></p>

<script>
  function getLocation() {
    if (navigator.geolocation) {
      navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition(function(position) {
        var lat = position.coords.latitude;
        var lon = position.coords.longitude;
        document.getElementById('geo-output').innerText = "Lat: " + lat + " | Lon: " + lon;
      });
    } else {
      document.getElementById('geo-output').innerText = "Geolocation is not supported.";
    }
  }
</script>

title: HTML5 Inputs
content:
HTML5 Input Attributes

Smarter Forms

HTML5 introduced highly useful input attributes that drastically reduce the amount of JavaScript you have to write to handle forms.

New Input Features
<form>
  <!-- Placeholder text disappears when you type -->
  <input type="text" placeholder="Enter your full name" required><br><br>
  
  <!-- Min and Max restrict numeric input -->
  <label>Rate 1-10:</label>
  <input type="number" min="1" max="10"><br><br>
  
  <!-- Autocomplete helps browsers fill in data -->
  <input type="email" autocomplete="on" placeholder="Email address">
</form>

title: Form Validation
content:
HTML5 Form Validation

Built-in Checking

HTML5 provides automatic, built-in form validation. If an input with a required attribute is empty, or an email input doesn't contain an '@' symbol, the browser will stop the form from submitting automatically!

Validation Example
<form action="/submit">
  <label>Email:</label>
  <input type="email" required>
  
  <br><br>
  <input type="submit" value="Try to Submit!">
</form>

title: Multimedia APIs
content:
HTML5 Multimedia APIs

Controlling Media

HTML5 allows you to take complete control over audio and video elements using JavaScript, allowing you to build your own custom media players.

Custom Video Buttons
<video id="myVideo" width="300">
  <source src="https://www.w3schools.com/html/mov_bbb.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>
<br>
<button onclick="document.getElementById('myVideo').play()">Play</button>
<button onclick="document.getElementById('myVideo').pause()">Pause</button>

title: Accessibility
content:
HTML5 Accessibility

Web for Everyone

Accessibility (a11y) ensures that websites are usable by all people, including those with disabilities relying on screen readers.



    <li><strong>Use alt attributes:</strong> Always describe images (e.g., <code><img src="img.jpg" alt="A brown dog"></code>).</li> <li><strong>Semantic HTML:</strong> Use <code><nav></code> and <code><main></code> to help screen readers navigate.</li> <li><strong>ARIA Roles:</strong> Use ARIA attributes to provide extra context when standard HTML isn't enough.</li> </ul>

title: HTML5 APIs
content:
HTML5 APIs Overview

Modern Web Capabilities

HTML5 provides powerful Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) that allow web browsers to do things that used to require native desktop apps. These APIs bridge the gap between simple web pages and complex web applications.

    <li><strong>Geolocation API:</strong> Find user coordinates.</li> <li><strong>Web Storage API:</strong> Save data locally.</li> <li><strong>Drag and Drop API:</strong> Move elements interactively.</li> <li><strong>Canvas & SVG:</strong> Draw 2D and 3D graphics.</li> </ul>

title: Web Workers
content:
HTML Web Workers

Background Threads

Normally, JavaScript runs on a single thread. If a script takes a long time to run, the webpage becomes unresponsive. Web Workers allow JavaScript to run in the background, independent of other scripts, without affecting the performance of the page.



Web Worker Setup
<script>
  // Check if Web Workers are supported
  if (window.Worker) {
    // Create a new worker connected to an external JS file
    var myWorker = new Worker('worker.js');
    
    // Send a message to start the background task
    myWorker.postMessage('Start processing');
  }
</script>

title: Server-Sent Events
content:
HTML Server-Sent Events

One-Way Messaging

Server-Sent Events (SSE) allow a web page to receive automatic, real-time updates from a server. This is perfect for live news feeds, stock tickers, or sports scores, where the server pushes data to the client without the client having to ask for it.

SSE Example
<h3>Live Updates:</h3>
<div id="result"></div>

<script>
  if (typeof(EventSource) !== "undefined") {
    var source = new EventSource('server.php');
    source.onmessage = function(event) {
      document.getElementById('result').innerHTML += event.data + "<br>";
    };
  } else {
    document.getElementById('result').innerHTML = "Sorry, your browser does not support SSE.";
  }
</script>

title: WebSockets
content:
HTML WebSockets

Real-Time Communication

While SSE is one-way (server to client), WebSockets enable a persistent, two-way (full-duplex) communication channel between the client and the server. This is the technology powering live chat applications and multiplayer browser games.



WebSocket Connection
<script>
  // Connect to a WebSocket server
  var socket = new WebSocket('ws://example.com/socket');
  
  // When the connection opens, send a message
  socket.onopen = function() {
    socket.send('Hello Server, I am connected!');
  };
  
  // Listen for messages coming back from the server
  socket.onmessage = function(event) {
    console.log('Server says: ', event.data);
  };
</script>

title: IndexedDB
content:
HTML IndexedDB

Advanced Local Storage

localStorage is great for saving small strings of text, but IndexedDB is a low-level API for storing massive amounts of structured data, including files and blobs. It functions like a real NoSQL database right inside your browser.

Opening a Database
<script>
  // Request to open a database named 'MyDatabase' (version 1)
  var request = indexedDB.open('MyDatabase', 1);
  
  request.onsuccess = function(event) {
    var db = event.target.result;
    console.log('Database successfully opened: ', db);
  };
  
  request.onerror = function(event) {
    console.error('Database error: ', event.target.errorCode);
  };
</script>

title: PWAs
content:
Progressive Web Apps (PWA)

Apps in the Browser

Progressive Web Apps (PWAs) are web applications built with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript that look and behave like native mobile apps downloaded from an App Store.



    <li><strong>Offline Support:</strong> Using Service Workers, PWAs can function even without an internet connection.</li> <li><strong>Installable:</strong> Users can "Add to Home Screen" directly from the browser.</li> <li><strong>Push Notifications:</strong> PWAs can send native device notifications.</li> </ul>

title: SEO Basics
content:
HTML SEO Basics

Search Engine Optimization

SEO helps search engines like Google understand your webpage so it ranks higher in search results. Your HTML structure is the absolute foundation of good SEO.

SEO Tags
<head>
  <!-- A clear, unique title is the #1 SEO factor -->
  <title>Learn HTML5: The Ultimate Guide for Beginners</title>
  
  <!-- The description appears under your link in Google results -->
  <meta name="description" content="Master HTML5 from scratch with our interactive, step-by-step tutorial.">
  
  <!-- Viewport ensures Google ranks you well for mobile users -->
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
</head>

title: Optimization
content:
Performance Optimization

Building Fast Sites

Users abandon slow websites. Structuring your HTML and assets correctly is vital for performance.

    <li><strong>Minimize HTTP Requests:</strong> Combine CSS and JS files where possible.</li> <li><strong>Optimize Images:</strong> Compress images and use modern formats like WebP. Add <code>loading="lazy"</code> to images below the fold.</li> <li><strong>External Files:</strong> Use external CSS and JS files instead of inline code so the browser can cache them.</li> <li><strong>Script Placement:</strong> Put <code><script></code> tags at the bottom of the <code><body></code> or use the <code>defer</code> attribute so they don't block the HTML from loading.</li> </ul>

title: Security
content:
HTML Security Best Practices

Protecting Your Users

As a web developer, you must build sites that are secure against hackers and malicious scripts.

    <li><strong>Prevent XSS (Cross-Site Scripting):</strong> Never trust user input. Always sanitize data before displaying it on the page so hackers can't inject malicious <code><script></code> tags.</li> <li><strong>Validate Input:</strong> Use HTML5 form validation (like <code>type="email"</code> and <code>required</code>), but ALWAYS re-validate the data on the backend server.</li> <li><strong>Use HTTPS:</strong> Serve your website over an encrypted HTTPS connection.</li> <li><strong>Avoid Inline Scripts:</strong> Keep your JavaScript in external files and enforce a strict Content Security Policy (CSP).</li> </ul>

title: Ultimate Summary
content:
HTML Complete Course Summary 🚀

You Did It! 🎉

You have successfully completed the HTML course! You now know how to build the structure and skeleton of any website on the internet.

  • Core Elements: Headings, Paragraphs, Links, and Images.
  • Structure & Semantics: Lists, Tables, Forms, and Semantic HTML5 elements.
  • Multimedia integrations: Audio, Video, Canvas, and SVG elements.
  • Advanced HTML5: Web Storage, Geolocation, Web Workers, and WebSockets.

Your structural foundation is solid! Now, let's learn how to make it beautiful. Let's dive into CSS!
